coredata.dc:title: An azamacrocycle functionalized GaAs (100) optical sensor for copper ion (II) detection in phosphate buffered saline solution
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xiaohuan
Xia, Peng
Liu, Biyun
Huang, Hua
coredata.dc:description: Copper ion is important in natural and biological processes. The present work describes the fabrication of a hybrid GaAs sensor for the label-free detection of copper ion in the near infrared region. The implemented sensing strategy relies on the sensitivity of the GaAs photoluminescence (PL) emission to the local environment at its surface. Specifically, GaAs (001) substrates were chemically passivated with carboxylic acid group terminated thiols, followed by the surface functionalization with artificial Me2Cyclen cavity by a classical EDC/NHS coupling reaction. Due to a specific recognition between Me2Cyclen and copper ions, CuII were coordinately captured onto the surface. The positive charges tended to deplete electrons from the near surface region and increase the band bending, leading to a PL decrease. The selectivity of the chip towards metallic ions was further confirmed by a combination of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, water contact angle measurements and AFM analysis. Collectively, the results described within suggest the promise of integrating well-designed organic scaffold with the unique optical properties of III–V semiconductors for a label-free detection of various analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuII detection
Surface functionalization
Photoluminescence
Azamacrocyclic cavity
GaAs (001)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of 11-MUA/1-PT mixed self-assembled monolayer (SAM) on GaAs
Immobilization of Me2Cyclen on carboxylic acids terminated GaAs
Binding and selectivity towards metallic ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL spectral responses of Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs before (ref)/after incubated in 50μM Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, K+ and Na+ (PBS buffer, pH=7.4), respectively.

a) PL spectral responses of the Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs to the addition of CuII; the concentration of CuII: 0, 5μM, 10μM, 20μM, 50μM, 0.1mM, 0.5mM, 2mM, 5mM and 10mM; b) The linear range of the relative PL intensities under low Cu2+ concentrations. The error bars correspond to the standard deviation of PL measurements across three experiments.

Series (a) showing relative PL intensity of Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs (001) after interaction with various metal ions (50μM) and series (b) showing competitive selectivity towards Cu2+ in the presence of other coexisting metal ions (50μM).
Profiles of water droplets on the Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs substrates before and after treated with different metal ions, respectively.
XPS survey spectra of Me2Cyclen functionalized chips treated with CuII (a), NiII (b) and ZnII (c), respectively.
High resolution As 3d and Ga 3d spectra of Me2Cyclen functionalized chip before (a) and after treated with CuII (b).
AFM images and cross-sectional profiles along the indicated lines of Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs (001) before (a) and after treated with CuII (b).
The synthesis route of Me2Cyclen (1) ligand.
Proposed mechanism for the capture of CuII on Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs (100) surface.

head.ce:title: An azamacrocycle functionalized GaAs (100) optical sensor for copper ion (II) detection in phosphate buffered saline solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis and Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis and Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper ion is important in natural and biological processes. The present work describes the fabrication of a hybrid GaAs sensor for the label-free detection of copper ion in the near infrared region. The implemented sensing strategy relies on the sensitivity of the GaAs photoluminescence (PL) emission to the local environment at its surface. Specifically, GaAs (001) substrates were chemically passivated with carboxylic acid group terminated thiols, followed by the surface functionalization with artificial Me2Cyclen cavity by a classical EDC/NHS coupling reaction. Due to a specific recognition between Me2Cyclen and copper ions, CuII were coordinately captured onto the surface. The positive charges tended to deplete electrons from the near surface region and increase the band bending, leading to a PL decrease. The selectivity of the chip towards metallic ions was further confirmed by a combination of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, water contact angle measurements and AFM analysis. Collectively, the results described within suggest the promise of integrating well-designed organic scaffold with the unique optical properties of III–V semiconductors for a label-free detection of various analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of copper ions has currently attracted a huge amount of research interest owing to the important roles of copper ions in environments, living systems and therapeutic agents [1,2]. Copper distribution in the human body is highly controlled. The lack of CuII can affect enzyme activity, leading to a wide variety of neurological problems, while the excess of CuII may cause the damage of the kidneys and liver [3]. In recent years, different categories of probes have been explored for CuII recognition, such as polymer based interface [4,5], gold nanocluster [6], carbon dots [3,7], gold electrode [8], transition metal complexes [9], metal-organic framework [10], silica nanoparticles [11], and chemosensors [12–14]. The detection methods include electrochemical measurements [15], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) assay [16], surface plasmon resonance [17], nanoparticle-based colorimetric assays [6], fluorescent spectroscopy [12], and so on. Among them, optical methods, especially fluorescence spectroscopy have been widely used due to their simplicity and high sensitivity [18,19]. Despite the promises, traditional fluorescent sensors suffer from several drawbacks, such as high susceptibility to photobleaching, low quantum yield in aqueous solution, and interference from the intrinsic fluorescence of biological tissues [20]. To overcome these limitations, great efforts have been devoted toward developing innovative label-free optical assays in the near-infrared (NIR) window (700nm to 1300nm), within which biological materials have minimal absorption [21].

Recent research interest has been extended to the design of novel hybrid devices by the integration of organic molecules with semiconductors, which tend to develop intelligent sensors that can detect the “local” binding and adsorption on the surface [22]. Gallium arsenide (GaAs), in particular, is an attractive material for electronic and photonic devices with high electron mobility and direct band gap [23,24]. The photoluminescence (PL) signal of GaAs in the NIR range is reported to be remarkably sensitive to surficial physical and chemical states, which is a highly promising candidate in the development of a new generation of optical sensors. At the same time, by the deposition of organic self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) to make the semiconductor biocompatible, GaAs based sensors have been reported for label-free detection of DNA [25], aptamer [26], bacteria detection [27,28] in the NIR window.

In this paper, the fabrication and operation of a hybrid GaAs based sensor for label-free NIR optical detection of copper ion are described. The solid-state sensing platform was prepared by functionalizing a GaAs substrate with carboxylic acid terminal thiols as the first step. Then the organic ligand, 1,7-dimethyl-1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane (Me2Cyclen) was anchored on the surface as a receptor, which exhibited exceptional binding ability towards transitional metal ions by coordinate bond [29,30]. The recognition ability of the chips towards metallic ions was investigated by photoluminescence (PL), which is known to be extremely sensitive to the deplete electrons from the near surface region of the GaAs [24]. From a fundamental perspective, the coordination process between the metallic ion and Me2Cyclen ligand directly affects the charge distribution on the surface, which increases the band bending, resulting in an increase of the width of the nonemissive depletion layer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and water contact angle measurements were also investigated to study the recognition ability of the chips towards CuII.

The Si-doped n-type (n=2×1017 cm−3) GaAs (100) with two-side polished substrates were purchased from AXT, Inc. 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (11-MUA), 1-Pentanethiol (1-PT), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydrosuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A degassed PBS solution and ethanol solution (typically 250mL) were prepared by flushing with a 3 SCFH high-purity nitrogen stream for 4h to remove residual oxygen. All other reagents were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification.

As shown in Scheme 1 , compound 2 (1.00g, 1.97mmol) was detosylated with sulphuric acid (10mL) at 110°C for 40h according to the literature [31]. The resulting amine sulphate was dissolved in water, and made alkaline with sodium hydroxide solution. The amine was extracted with 10mL chloroform for three times, and the combined extracts were dried with Na2SO4. The crude product was obtained after the evaporation of chloroform under reduced pressure, which was purified by column chromatography (SiO2, CH2Cl2/MeOH, 10:1) to give Me2Cyclen (1) (0.28g, yield: 70%). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 300MHz) δ (ppm): 5.94 (s, br, 4H, NH), 2.68–2.71 (m, 8H, NH CH2 ), 2.46–2.50 (m, 8H, NCH2), 2.23 (s, 6H, CH3). ESI–MS m/z 201.00 [MH]+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of 11-MUA/1-PT mixed self-assembled monolayer (SAM) on GaAs
ce:section.ce:para: n-GaAs (100) wafers were first cleaved into small pieces (5mm×5mm) and then sequentially cleaned by Milli-Q water, ethyl alcohol, acetone through 5min sonication in each solvent. Prior to self-assembled monolayer (SAM) formation, the native oxides such as Ga2O3, As2O5, and As2O3 were removed by immersion in a 2M HCl solution for 5min followed by a 3min deionized water rinse [32]. The samples were incubated at room temperature in a mixture of 11-MUA/1-PT (1:5) diluted in the deoxygenized ethanol at a final concentration of 2mM for 20h, followed by thorough rinse with ethanol to get rid of superfluous thiol molecules physically adsorbed to the substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of Me2Cyclen on carboxylic acids terminated GaAs
ce:section.ce:para: Me2Cyclens were immobilized on the surface through the carbodiimide mediated reaction with carboxylic acids terminal groups. To ensure covalent binding, SAM coated samples were immersed for 30min in mixed NHS (0.1M) and EDC (0.4M). After activation, unreacted NHS and EDC were removed by rinsing with deionized (DI) water, followed by the exposure of samples to 2mM Me2Cyclens aqueous solution for 30min. Then the sample was rinsed with water thoroughly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding and selectivity towards metallic ions
ce:section.ce:para: The detection targets (metallic ion solutions) were prepared by dissolving the salt of Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, K+ and Na+ in 10mM phosphate buffer (PBS, pH=7.4) to a concentration of 50μM. The final solution was sonicated for 5min before use. In order to detect the recognition ability of the functionalized chips towards metallic ions, the hybridized Me2Cyclen-GaAs chip was incubated in metallic salt solutions (PBS, pH=7.4) for 30min, followed by thorough rinse with DI water before characterization.

PL spectra of GaAs were collected using a Renishaw inVia Reflex Raman Microscope, with the laser-pumping wavelength at 532nm, and the acquisition time is 10s. The excitation power density was 0.01mW/cm2. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on a K-Alpha (1486.68eV) X-ray sources (USA). Contact angle measurements were carried out by using a Rame-Hart NRL Model 100 goniometer at room temperature and ambient humidity. A minimum of two independent measurements were made for each surface (5 mm×5mm). The static angles reported are the averages of more than three batches of samples, and the measurements were done within 20s following contact. The surface morphology of processed samples was investigated by using a Nanoscope IIIa microscope (Digital Instruments, Inc.) operating in a tapping mode. The cantilever spring constant and the resonance frequency were 40N/m and 300kHz, respectively. All the AFM measurements were carried out in an air environment. The root-mean-square roughness (RMS) values of the investigated surfaces refer to an area of 5.0μm×5.0μm.

Cyclen derivatives feature prominently due to their strong binding ability towards metallic ions, which continue attracting the interest of organic, inorganic, and biochemists [29,30,33]. Herein, Me2Cyclen ligand was anchored to the surface and its recognition abilities towards metallic ions were investigated. Under laser-pumping wavelength at 532nm, the near-infrared (NIR) PL band observed at around 860nm is consistent with the band gap of n-GaAs. The selective recognition ability of the functionalized GaAs towards various metal ions, such as Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, K+ and Na+ (PBS buffer, pH=7.4) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 1 , after exposure to 50μM various metal ion solutions for 30min, there is a significant PL decrease for the chip treated with CuII. While under the same condition, other metal ions such as Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, K+ and Na+ show quite few effect on the PL intensity of the hybrid GaAs sample.

In the presence of CuII, the PL intensity of the system was gradually decreased with increasing concentrations of CuII (Fig. 2 a). When CuII ions were captured by Me2Cyclen cavity, the positive charge depletes electrons from the near surface region, resulting in an increase of the band bending and the width of the depletion layer [34]. Since light-generated charge carriers created within a distance on the order of the depletion width do not contribute to the luminescence signal, an increase in depletion width corresponds to a decrease in the measured PL intensity [24,26]. Fig. 2b shows the PL peak intensity as a function of CuII concentration ranging from 0 to 50μM. On the basis of the definition that the detection of limit (LOD) is the lowest analyte concentration required to produce a signal at 3 times the standard deviation of the background (S/N=3), the LOD for this system is determined to be ∼2μM, which is lower than the copper ions level (20μM) of drinking water defined by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).

ESI mass spectrometry was used to determine the stoichiometry of the complex formed by Me2Cyclen cavities and Cu2+ (Figs. S1, S2). For the Me2Cyclen ligand (m/z at 201.00), upon interaction with 50μM Cu2+, a peak m/z at 298.12 with a 100% relative abundance may be ascribed to [Cu(Me2Cyclen)(H2O)(OH−)]+, indicating the formation of 1:1 complex. The stability constants Ka of Me2Cyclen-CuII complexes was determined by the Benesi-Hildebrand equation [35,36]:
(1)
1
Δ P L
=
1
Δ P L
s a t
+
1
Δ P L
s a t
K a
[
C u
I I
]

where is photoluminescence difference at 860nm and is the maximum photoluminescence difference at 860nm. The association constant Ka was evaluated graphically by plotting 1/ΔPL against 1/[CuII]. Data were linearly fitted according to equation (1) and the Ka value was obtained from the slope and intercept of the line (Fig. S3). The Ka value of Me2Cyclen-CuII complexes was 1.45×105 M−1 (R2 =0.992), which is consistent with the strong binding affinity of cyclen cavity for CuII as reported before [37,38]. Moreover, interference of coexisting ions did not induce a significant effect on the recognition behavior of Cu2+ ion as shown in Fig. 3 . Such results make us believe that this method could become a potential CuII probe under biological conditions in NIR range. The chip exhibits a good selectivity towards CuII, because the size of the CuII cation matches that of the macrocyclic cavity. The yielding complexes are the metal ions invariably coordinated in the plane formed by the four nitrogen donors of Me2Cyclen. The proposed mechanism for the CuII-responsive PL change on the functionalized chip is shown in Scheme 2 .

The recognition ability of the functionalized surface towards metallic ions was further investigated by a static water contact angle measurement. The functionalized GaAs chip with Me2Cyclen ligands gave a shiny hydrophilic surface with the contact angle 64±2°, which was reduced to 31±2° after treated with 50μM CuII (PBS buffer, pH=7.4) (Fig. 4 ). For the samples treated with ZnII, the contact angle was slightly decreased (49±2°). After treated with other metal ions, such as Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, K+ and Na+, the contact angles were 60±1°, 52±2°, 62±1°, 54±2°, 68±1°, 65±2°, 56±1°, 59±2° and 62±2°, respectively. Obviously, the most significant contact angle reduction was induced by CuII, indicating that Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs had a good selectivity towards CuII. The results were consistent with stable complex formed between Me2Cyclen and CuII as reported before [29,30]. When the positively charged copper was trapped in Me2Cyclen cavity, the polarity at the interface was increased, which triggered a significant wettability switching on the functionalized chip [39]. Thus, these results corroborate the PL observations concerning the excellent selectivity of the chip towards CuII.

The XPS surveys in Fig. 5 compare the surface compositions of Me2Cyclen functionalized GaAs chips that were treated with 50μM CuII, ZnII and NiII (PBS buffer, pH=7.4), respectively. Firstly, for all of the three, in addition to the signal of Ga, As, C, O and S elements, the existence of N 1s peak suggests that Me2Cyclen was immobilized onto the surface. Secondly, for the one treated with CuII, a significant peak of Cu 2p 3/2 at 933eV can be seen in Fig. 5a [40], and the atomic percentage of copper is 0.8%. While for the one treated with ZnII, the peak of Zn 2p 3/2 at 1022eV is relatively weak and its atomic percentage is only 0.14% (Fig. 5b). The signal of NiII 2p 3/2 at 855eV was undetectable for the one treated with NiII PBS solution (Fig. 5c). These results indicate that compared to ZnII and NiII, more CuII were captured by the functionalized chip.

The high resolution As 3d and Ga 3d spectra were investigated to further compare the surface components before and after treated with CuII (Fig. 6 ). The measured XPS spectra were decomposed into individual components and fitted self-consistently with a series of doublets and fitting parameters chosen by using the full set of spectra. The results of our previous work on GaAs chips were taken into account to verify the fitting parameters [41,42]. For the sample before treated with CuII, both Ga 3d and As 3d spectra exhibit the dominant Ga to As binding peak, and a small amount of Ga2O, Ga2O3, As0 and As2O3 were observed (Fig. 6a). According to theoretical calculations, the formation of 11-MUA/1-PT mixed SAM covers only part of the available surface sites [43]. The residual empty Ga and As dangling bond is sensitive to the oxygen in the environment, which is for the reason that a small amount of Ga2O3 and As2O3 are on the functionalized chip [44]. Due to the surface states potential lies above the redox potential of Cu/CuII couple [45], electron transfer from the surface state to CuIIis thermodynamically possible, which generates more oxide species. Thus, a huge amount of As2O3 and Ga2O3 were observed on the chip (b) after treated with CuII (Fig. 6b). The growths of surface oxides always increase the surface state and have detrimental effects on electronic properties of the interface [10], which explains the CuII – triggered PL decrease reported before.

AFM was introduced to further confirm copper binding process on the hybrid GaAs surface. For hybrid GaAs sample before and after interacting with copper, the AFM measurements were performed in three separate sites of the 5.0μm×5.0μm areas. The root-mean-square roughness amplitudes (σRMS) of the hybrid sample before and after treated with CuII are 1.18±0.3 and 3.67±0.5nm as shown in Fig. 7 . The surface roughness is increased after exposed to CuII, which may be due to the conformational change of the organic moleculesand surface oxidation during CuII binding process.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a novel organic cavity hybridized GaAs (100) sensing platform for the NIR photoluminescence detection of CuII under physiological condition. The functionalized chip was achieved by surface passivation with carboxylic acid terminal thiols, followed by anchoring artificial Me2Cyclen ligand the surface. The positively charged CuII ion was trapped onto the interface by its strong coordinate binding with Me2Cyclen, which induces a decrease of PL emission from GaAs by depleting electrons from the near surface region. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and water contact angle measurements indicate the specificity and selectivity of the hybrid sensor in identifying CuII. To the best of our knowledge, it is a rare report about the fabrication of III–V semiconductor sensors by immobilizing well-designed organic scaffolds as binding sites for metallic ions detection. The present work may provide a new avenue for the smart design and fabrication of various highly efficient NIR optical sensors for environmental and biological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) [Grant No. 21501055] for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.039.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Development of a highly selective fluorescence probe for hydrogen sulfide

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaohuan Huang received her MSc and PhD degrees from the Department of Chemistry and Molecular Science at Wuhan University and pursued her postdoctoral studies at Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Université de Sherbrooke, Canada. Her research interest focuses on the fabrication of III–V semiconductors based sensors.
